2.1: When the Lord took Elijah up to heaven in a whirlwind, Elijah was leaving Gilgal with Elisha.
2.2: Elijah said to Elisha, «Please stay here, for the Lord has sent me to Bethel.» Elisha replied, «As surely as the Lord lives and as you live, I will not leave you.» So they went down to Bethel.
2.3: The sons of the prophets who were at Bethel went out to Elisha and said to him, «Do you know that the Lord will take your master from you today?» He replied, «Yes, I know; be at peace.»
2.4: Elijah said to him, «Elisha, please stay here, for the Lord has sent me to Jericho.» He replied, «As surely as the Lord lives and as you live, I will not leave you.» And they arrived at Jericho.
2.5: The sons of the prophets who were at Jericho approached Elisha and said to him, «Do you know that the Lord will take your master from you today?» He replied, «Yes, I know; be at peace.»
2.6: Elijah said to him, «Stay here, I beg you, for the Lord has sent me to the Jordan.» He replied, «As surely as the Lord lives and as you live, I will not leave you.» And they both went away.
2.7: Fifty men from among the sons of the prophets went after them and stood at a distance, on the opposite side, and the two of them stopped at the edge of the Jordan.
2.8: Then Elijah took his cloak, rolled it up, and struck the waters, and they parted to one side and to the other, and the two of them crossed over on dry ground.
2.9: When they had crossed over, Elijah said to Elisha, «Ask what I should do for you before I am taken from you.» Elisha replied, «May a double portion of your spirit come upon me?»
2.10: Elijah said, «You have asked a difficult thing. If you see me while I am being taken from you, it will be so for you; if not, it will not be so.»
2.11: They continued walking and talking together, and suddenly a chariot of fire and horses of fire separated them from each other, and Elijah went up to heaven in a whirlwind.
2.12: Elisha watched and cried out, «My father, my father, the chariot of Israel and its horsemen!» But he saw him no more. Then he took hold of his clothes and tore them in two pieces,
2.13: And he picked up Elijah's mantle, which had fallen off him. Then, returning to the Jordan, he stood on the bank,
2.14: And taking the mantle of Elijah that had fallen from him, he struck the waters with it and said, «Where is the Lord, the God of Elijah? Where is he?» When he had struck the waters, they parted to one side and to the other, and Elisha crossed over.
2.15: The sons of the prophets who were at Jericho, on the other side, saw him and said, "The spirit of Elijah rests on Elisha," and they went out to meet him and bowed down to the ground before him.
2.16: They said to him, «Look, there are fifty valiant men among your servants; let them go and seek your master. Perhaps the Spirit of the Lord has carried him away and thrown him down on one of the mountains or into one of the valleys.» He replied, «Do not send them.»
2.17: But they pressed him until he was embarrassed, and he said to them, "Send them." So they sent fifty men, who searched for Elijah for three days, but did not find him.
2.18: When they returned to Elisha, for he was staying in Jericho, he said to them, «Did I not tell you, »Do not go’?”
2.19: The people of the city said to Elisha, «The city is well situated, as my lord sees, but the waters are bad and the land is barren.»
2.20: He said, "Bring me a new bowl and put some salt in it." And they brought it to him.
2.21: He went to the spring of water and, having thrown salt into it, said, «Thus says the Lord: I have healed this water; from now on neither death nor barrenness will come from it.»
2.22: And the waters were purified to this day, according to the word that Elisha had spoken.
2.23: From there he went up to Bethel, and as he was going up the road, some little boys came out of the town and mocked him, saying, "Go up, bald man! Go up, bald man!"«
2.24: He turned to look at them and cursed them in the name of the Lord. And two bears came out of the forest and tore forty-two of those children to pieces.
2.25: From there he went to Mount Carmel and from there he returned to Samaria.

3.1: Joram, son of Ahab, became king of Israel in Samaria in the eighteenth year of Jehoshaphat, king of Judah, and he reigned twelve years.
3.2: He did what was evil in the sight of the Lord, but not like his father and mother, for he removed the pillar of Baal, which his father had made.
3.3: But he clung to the sins of Jeroboam, son of Nebat, who had caused Israel to sin, and he did not turn away from them.
3.4: Mesha, king of Moab, owned flocks and he paid the king of Israel a tribute of one hundred thousand lambs and one hundred thousand rams with their fleeces.
3.5: Upon Ahab's death, the king of Moab revolted against the king of Israel.
3.6: King Joram therefore went out that day from Samaria and surveyed all Israel.
3.7: Having set out, he sent word to Jehoshaphat, king of Judah: «The king of Moab has rebelled against me; will you come with me to attack Moab?» Jehoshaphat replied: «I will go up; it will be to me as to you, to my people as to your people, to my horses as to your horses.»
3.8: And he said, «By which route shall we go up?» Joram replied, «By the way of the desert of Edom.»
3.9: The king of Israel, the king of Judah, and the king of Edom set out. After a seven-day march, there was no water for the army or for the animals that followed it.
3.10: Then the king of Israel said, «Alas! The Lord has called these three kings to deliver them into the hands of Moab.»
3.11: But Jehoshaphat said, «Is there no prophet of the Lord here, through whom we may inquire of the Lord?» One of the servants of the king of Israel answered and said, «Elisha, the son of Shaphat, is here; he used to pour water on Elijah’s hands.»
3.12: And Jehoshaphat said, "The word of the Lord is with him." The king of Israel, Jehoshaphat, king of Judah, and the king of Edom went down to him.
3.13: Elisha said to the king of Israel, «What do you want with me? Go to the prophets of your father and to the prophets of your mother.» And the king of Israel said to him, «No, for the Lord has called these three kings to deliver them into the hand of Moab.»
3.14: Elisha said, «As surely as the Lord Almighty lives, before whom I stand, if I did not respect Jehoshaphat king of Judah, I would not pay any attention to you or even look at you.”.
3.15: "Now bring me a harpist." And while the harpist was playing, the hand of the Lord was upon Elisha,
3.16: And he said, «Thus says the Lord: Make in this valley pits and pits.
3.17: For thus says the Lord: You will see no wind and you will see no rain, and this valley will be filled with water, and you, your flocks and your beasts of burden will drink.
3.18: But this is a small thing in the eyes of the Lord: he will deliver Moab into your hands.
3.19: You will attack all the fortified cities and all the important cities, you will cut down all the fruit trees, you will block up all the springs of water, and you will devastate all the best fields by covering them with stones.»
3.20: In fact, in the morning, at the time when the offering was made, behold, water came by way of Edom, and the land was filled with water.
3.21: However, when all the Moabites learned that the kings were coming up to attack them, all the men capable of wearing a baldric and even more were summoned and they took up positions on the border.
3.22: They rose early in the morning and, when the sun shone on the waters, the Moabites saw before them the waters red like blood.
3.23: They said, "This is bloodshed. The kings are destroyed; they have struck one another, and now the Moabites are plundered."«
3.24: And they advanced toward the camp of Israel. But the Israelites rose up and attacked Moab, who fled before them. Entering the land, they attacked Moab,
3.25: They destroyed the cities, each throwing their stone into all the best fields, they filled them with it, they blocked all the springs of water, they cut down all the fruit trees, to the point that of Qir-Charoseth only the stones remained, the slingers had surrounded and beaten it.
3.26: When the king of Moab saw that he was losing the battle, he took with him seven hundred men, with drawn swords, to fight his way to the king of Edom, but they could not succeed.
3.27: Then he took his firstborn son, who was to reign in his place, and offered him as a burnt offering on the wall. And great indignation seized Israel, and they withdrew from the king of Moab and returned to their own land.

4.1: A woman, one of the wives of the sons of the prophets, cried out to Elisha, saying, «Your servant, my husband, is dead, and you know that your servant feared the Lord. Now the creditor has come and taken my two children to be his slaves.»
4.2: Elisha said to her, «What can I do for you? Tell me, what do you have in the house?» She replied, «Your servant has nothing at all in the house except a jar of oil.»
4.3: He said, «Go outside and ask all your neighbors for empty vessels, and not just a few.
4.4: When you get home, shut the door behind you and your children, pour some of your oil into all these jars, and set aside those that are full.»
4.5: So she left him. She closed the door on herself and her children; they brought the vases to her and she poured.
4.6: When the jars were full, she said to her son, "Bring me another jar." But he replied, "There is no more jar." And the oil stopped flowing.
4.7: She went and told the man of God, and he said, «Go, sell the oil and pay your debt, and you and your sons can live on what remains.»
4.8: One day Elisha was passing through Shunem. There was a rich woman there, who urged him to accept a meal, and every time he passed by, he went to her house to eat.
4.9: She said to her husband, "Look, I know that he is a holy man of God, who often comes to our house like this.
4.10: Let's make him a small, high room against the wall and put in it a bed, a table, a chair, and a candlestick, so that he can retire there when he comes to our house.»
4.11: Elisha, having returned another day to Shunem, retired to the upper room and slept there.
4.12: He said to Giezi, his servant, "Call this Shunammite woman." Giezi called her, and she came before him.
4.13: And Elisha said to Giez, «Tell her, »You have shown us all this concern: what can be done for you? Should we speak to the king or the commander of the army on your behalf?«» She answered Giez, “I live among my own people.”
4.14: And Elisha said, "What can be done for her?" Giezi replied, "But she has no son, and her husband is old."«
4.15: And Elisha said, "Call her." Giezi called her, and she stood at the door.
4.16: And Elisha said to her, «At this time next year, you will have a son.» And she said, «No, my lord, man of God, do not deceive your servant.»
4.17: And the woman conceived and gave birth to a son at the same time the following year, just as Elisha had told her.
4.18: The child grew up. One day, when he had gone to find his father among the harvesters,
4.19: He said to his father, "My head! My head!" The father said to his servant, "Take him to his mother."«
4.20: The servant having taken him away and brought him to his mother, the child remained on his mother's lap until noon, then he died.
4.21: She went upstairs, laid him on the bed of the man of God, closed the door on him and left.
4.22: She called her husband and said, "Please send me one of the servants and one of the donkeys, so that I may run to the man of God and return immediately.".
4.23: He said, "Why go to him today? It is neither the new moon nor the Sabbath." She replied, "Don't worry."«
4.24: And, having saddled the donkey, she said to her servant, "Lead me on and go, do not stop me on the way unless I tell you to."«
4.25: She left and went to the man of God on Mount Carmel. When the man of God saw her from afar, he said to Giezhi, his servant, «Here is the Shunammite woman.
4.26: So run to meet her and say to her, »Are you well? Is your husband well? Is your child well?« She replied, »Well.”
4.27: As soon as she arrived at the man of God's place on the mountain, she seized his feet. Giezi approached to push her away, but the man of God said, "Leave her alone, for her soul is in bitterness, and the Lord has hidden it from me and has not revealed it to me."«
4.28: Then she said, "Did I ask my lord for a son? Did I not say, 'Do not deceive me'?"«
4.29: And Elisha said to Giez, «Gird up your loins, take my staff in your hand and go. If you meet anyone, do not greet him, and if anyone greets you, do not answer him. You shall lay my staff on the face of the child.»
4.30: The child's mother said, "As the Lord lives and as your soul lives, I will not leave you." And Elisha got up and followed her.
4.31: Giezi had gone ahead and placed the staff on the child's face, but there was no sound, no sign of attention. He returned to meet Elisha and reported the matter, saying, "The child has not woken up."«
4.32: When Elisha arrived at the house, behold, the child was dead, lying on his bed.
4.33: Elisha went in and, having closed the door behind them both, he prayed to the Lord.
4.34: And he went up and lay on the child, he put his mouth on his mouth, his eyes on his eyes, his hands on his hands, and he stretched himself out on him, and the child's flesh grew warm.
4.35: Elisha went away and walked to and fro in the house, then he climbed back onto the bed and lay down on the child and the child sneezed seven times and the child opened his eyes.
4.36: Elisha called Giez and said, "Call the Shunammite woman." Giez called her, and she came to Elisha, who said to her, "Take your son."«
4.37: She came and fell at his feet and prostrated herself on the ground and, taking her son, she went out.
4.38: Elisha returned to Gilgal, and the famine was in the land. As the sons of the prophets were sitting before him, he said to his servant, «Put on the large pot and cook some stew for the sons of the prophets.»
4.39: One of them went out into the fields to gather herbs, found a kind of wild vine, and picked wild gourds, filling his garment. When he returned, he cut them into pieces and added them to the pot where the soup was being prepared, without their knowledge.
4.40: They poured soup for these men to eat, but as soon as they had eaten the soup, they cried out, saying, "Death is in the pot, man of God!" And they could not eat.
4.41: Elisha said, "Bring me some flour." He threw some into the jar and said, "Pour it out to the people so they may eat." And there was nothing bad left in the jar.
4.42: A man came from Baal-Salisah, bringing to the man of God some bread from the firstfruits, namely twenty barley loaves and some fresh wheat in his sack. Elisha said, «Give it to the people so they may eat.»
4.43: His servant replied, "How can I serve this in front of a hundred people?" But Elisha said, "Give it to the people so they can eat. For this is what the Lord says: 'They will eat and there will be some left over.'"«
4.44: So he placed the loaves before them and they ate and had some left over, according to the word of the Lord.

5.1: Naaman, commander of the army of the king of Syria, was a powerful and respected man in the sight of his master, for it was through him that the Lord had granted salvation to the Syrians, but this strong and valiant man was a leper.
5.2: But the Syrians, having gone out in bands, had taken captive a little girl from the land of Israel, who was in the service of Naaman's wife.
5.3: She said to her mistress, "Oh. If my lord were with the prophet who is in Samaria, the prophet would deliver him from his leprosy."«
5.4: Naaman came and reported this to his master, saying, "The young woman from the land of Israel spoke in such and such a way."«
5.5: And the king of Syria said, «Go, and I will send a letter to the king of Israel.» So he went, taking with him ten talents of silver, six thousand shekels of gold, and ten changes of clothing.
5.6: He brought to the king of Israel the letter which read: «Now therefore, when this letter reaches you, behold, I am sending you Naaman, my servant, so that you may cure him of his leprosy.»
5.7: After reading the letter, the king of Israel tore his clothes and said, «Am I a god, able to kill and to give life, that he should send me to cure a man of his leprosy? Know then and see that he is seeking a quarrel with me.»
5.8: When Elisha, the man of God, heard that the king of Israel had torn his clothes, he sent word to the king: «Why have you torn your clothes? Let him come to me and he will know that there is a prophet in Israel.»
5.9: Naaman came with his horses and chariot and stopped at the door of Elisha's house.
5.10: Elisha sent a messenger to him, saying, «Go and wash yourself seven times in the Jordan, and your flesh will be restored and you will be clean.»
5.11: Naaman was angry and went away, saying, «I thought he would surely come out to me and stand and call on the name of the Lord his God, and wave his hand over the sore and heal the leper.
5.12: Are not the Abana and Pharphar rivers of Damascus better than all the waters of Israel? Could I not wash in them and become clean?» And turning away, he went off in anger.
5.13: His servants approached him to speak to him and said, «My father, if the prophet had asked you to do something difficult, would you not have done it? How much more should you obey him when he tells you, »Wash and be clean’?”
5.14: He went down and dipped himself seven times in the Jordan, according to the word of the man of God, and his flesh became like the flesh of a little child and he was cleansed.
5.15: Naaman returned to the man of God with all his entourage. When he arrived, he stood before him and said, «Now I know that there is no God in all the earth except in Israel. So now accept a gift from your servant.»
5.16: Elisha replied, "As surely as the Lord lives before whom I stand, I will not accept it." Naaman urged him to accept, but he refused.
5.17: And Naaman said, «Otherwise, allow your servant to be given two mules’ loads of earth, for your servant will no longer offer burnt offerings or sacrifices to any other gods except the Lord.
5.18: However, may the Lord forgive your servant this: When my master enters the house of Remmon to worship and leans on my hand, and I also bow down in the house of Remmon, may the Lord forgive your servant if I bow down in the house of Remmon.»
5.19: Elisha said to him, «Go in peace.» And Naaman left Elisha. He was some distance away,
5.20: When Gizi, the servant of Elisha, the man of God, said to himself, «Look, my master has spared Naaman, this Syrian, by not accepting from his hand what he brought. As the Lord lives, I will run after him and get something from him.»
5.21: And Giezi began to pursue Naaman. When Naaman saw him running after him, he got down from his chariot to meet him and said, "Is everything all right?"«
5.22: Giezi replied, «All is well. My master sends me to tell you: Two young men from the hill country of Ephraim, descendants of the prophets, have just arrived at my house. Please give them a talent of silver and two changes of clothing.»
5.23: Naaman said, "Agree to take two talents." He urged him to accept and, having packed two talents of silver in two bags with two changes of clothes, he gave them to two of his servants to carry before Giezi.
5.24: Arriving at the hill, Giézi took them from their hands and placed them in the house, then he sent the men away, and they left.
5.25: And he went and presented himself to his master. Elisha said to him, «Where have you been, Giez?» He replied, «Your servant has gone neither to one side nor the other.»
5.26: But Elisha said to him, «Did not my spirit go with you when the man left his chariot to meet you? Is this the time to accept money and to accept clothing and olive trees and vineyards and sheep and oxen and male and female servants?
5.27: "Naaman's leprosy will cling to you and your descendants forever." And Giezi went out from Elisha's presence with leprosy as white as snow.

6.1: The sons of the prophets said to Elisha, «Look, the place where we are sitting before you is too small for us.
6.2: "Let's go to the Jordan, and each of us will take a beam and make ourselves a place to live in." Elisha replied, "Go."»
6.3: And one of them said, "Agree to come with your servants." He replied, "I will go.",
6.4: And he went with them. When they arrived at the Jordan, they cut wood.
6.5: As one of them was cutting down a beam, the iron fell into the water; he cried out and said, "Alas, my lord, and I had borrowed it."«
6.6: The man of God said, "Where did it fall?" And he showed him the place. Then Elisha cut a piece of wood, threw it into that spot, and the iron floated to the surface.
6.7: And he said, "Take it." He reached out his hand and took it.
6.8: The king of Syria was at war with Israel. After consulting with his servants, he said, "My camp will be in such and such a place."«
6.9: But the man of God sent word to the king of Israel: «Beware of crossing into that place, for the Syrians are coming down there.»
6.10: And the king of Israel sent people to the place that the man of God had told him about and pointed out to him, and he was on his guard there more than once.
6.11: The heart of the king of Syria was troubled by this maneuver; he summoned his servants and said to them, "Will you not tell me which of us is for the king of Israel?"«
6.12: One of his servants replied, "No one, O king, my lord, but Elisha the prophet, who is in Israel, reports to the king of Israel the words that you speak in your bedroom."«
6.13: The king said, "Go and see where he is, and I will send for him." They came and told him, "He is in Dothan."«
6.14: So he sent horses, chariots and a large troop, which arrived one night and surrounded the city.
6.15: The servant of the man of God got up early in the morning and went out, and behold, an army with horses and chariots surrounded the city. And the servant said to Elisha, «Ah, my lord, what shall we do?»
6.16: He replied, "Do not be afraid, for those who are with us are more than those who are with them."«
6.17: Elisha prayed and said, «Lord, open his eyes so that he may see.» And the Lord opened the servant’s eyes, and he saw, and behold, the mountain was full of horses and chariots of fire all around Elisha.
6.18: The Syrians went down to the man of God. Elisha prayed to the Lord and said, «Please strike this nation with blindness.» And the Lord struck them with blindness, according to the word of Elisha.
6.19: Elisha said to them, «This is not the way and this is not the city; follow me and I will lead you to the man you are looking for.» And he led them to Samaria.
6.20: When they had entered Samaria, Elisha said, «Lord, open the eyes of these people so that they may see.» And the Lord opened their eyes, and they saw, and behold, they were in the midst of Samaria.
6.21: When the king of Israel saw them, he said to Elisha, «Shall I strike them down, shall I strike them down, my father?»
6.22: And Elisha replied, «You shall not strike them. Those whom you have taken captive with your sword and bow, strike them, but place bread and water before these men, so that they may eat and drink and then go to their master.»
6.23: The king of Israel prepared a great feast for them, and they ate and drank. Then he sent them away, and they returned to their master. And the Syrian bands never returned to the territory of Israel.
6.24: After that, Benhadad, king of Syria, having gathered all his army, went up and besieged Samaria.
6.25: There was a great famine in Samaria and it was besieged so severely that a donkey's head was worth eighty shekels of silver and a quarter of a measure of pigeon dung was worth five shekels of silver.
6.26: As the king was passing over the wall, a woman cried out to him, saying, "Save me, O king, my lord."«
6.27: He said, «If the Lord does not save you, how can I save you? From the produce of the threshing floor or the winepress?»
6.28: And the king said to her, "What is the matter with you?" She said, "This woman told me: Give me your son, we will eat him today and tomorrow we will eat mine.".
6.29: So we cooked my son and ate him. And the next day I said to her, 'Give us your son and we will eat him.' But she hid her son."»
6.30: When the king heard the woman's words, he tore his clothes as he passed over the wall, and the people saw that he had sackcloth underneath, next to his skin.
6.31: The king said, "May God deal with me, be it ever so severely, if the head of Elisha, son of Shaphat, remains on him today."«
6.32: Now, while Elisha was sitting in his house and the elders were sitting with him, the king sent a messenger from him. But before the messenger arrived, Elisha said to the elders, «Do you know that this son of a murderer has sent someone to take my head? Be careful: when the messenger comes, shut the door and push him back with it. But can you not hear the sound of his master’s footsteps behind him?»
6.33: He was still speaking to them when the messenger came down to him and said, «This is disaster from the Lord; what hope is there for me from the Lord?»

7.1: Elisha said, «Hear the word of the Lord: This is what the Lord says: Tomorrow about this time, a quart of fine flour will sell for a shekel and two quarts of barley for a shekel at the gate of Samaria.»
7.2: The officer on whose arm the king was leaning answered the man of God and said, «Even if the Lord were to make windows in the heavens, could this happen?» Elisha said, «You will see it with your own eyes, but you will not eat of it.»
7.3: There were four lepers at the entrance of the gate, who said to one another, "Why should we stay here until we die?"
7.4: If we choose to enter the city, famine will strike and we will die there; if we stay here, we will also die. Come then, let us throw ourselves into the Syrian camp; if they spare our lives, we will live, and if they kill us, we will die.»
7.5: They got up at dusk to go to the Syrian camp and they arrived at the entrance of the Syrian camp and behold, there was no one there.
7.6: The Lord had caused the Syrians to hear the sound of chariots and horses, the sound of a great army, and they said to one another, "Look, the king of Israel has hired the kings of the Hittites and the kings of the Egyptians to come and attack us.".
7.7: And, getting up, they had fled at dusk, abandoning their tents, their horses and their donkeys, the camp as it was, and they had fled to save their lives.
7.8: The lepers, having arrived at the entrance to the camp, entered a tent and, after eating and drinking, they took silver, gold, and clothing, which they went and hid. They returned, entered another tent, and took more items, which they hid in the same way.
7.9: Then they said to one another, «We are not acting rightly. This day is a day of good news, but if we remain silent and wait until morning, we will face punishment. Come, then, let us go and tell the king’s household.»
7.10: They left and, having called the guards at the city gate, they reported to them: «We entered the Syrian camp and behold, there was no one there, nor any human voice, only tied horses, tied donkeys, and the tents just as they were.»
7.11: The gate guards shouted and carried the news inside the king's house.
7.12: The king got up during the night and said to his servants, «I want to tell you what the Syrians are doing to us. Knowing that we are hungry, they have left their camp to hide in the fields, and they said to themselves, »When they come out of the city, we will seize them alive and enter the city.’”
7.13: One of his servants spoke up and said, «Let five of the surviving horses that are still in the city be taken; behold, they are like all the multitude of Israel that remained there, behold, they are like all the multitude of Israel that is dying, and we will send to see.»
7.14: Two chariots with horses were taken, and the king sent men after the Syrian army, saying, "Go and see."«
7.15: They followed them as far as the Jordan, and behold, the whole road was covered with clothing and belongings that the Syrians had thrown away in their haste. On their return, the messengers reported everything to the king.
7.16: Immediately the people went out and plundered the Syrian camp, and they obtained a measure of fine flour for a shekel and two measures of barley for a shekel, according to the word of the Lord.
7.17: The king had entrusted the guard of the gate to the officer on whose hand he leaned, but this officer was trampled underfoot by the people and he died, according to the word that the man of God had spoken when the king came down to him.
7.18: Indeed, when the man of God had spoken to the king, saying, "Two measures of barley will be sold for a shekel, and one measure of fine flour for a shekel, tomorrow at this time, at the gate of Samaria,"«
7.19: The officer had answered the man of God and said, "Even if the Lord were to make windows in the heavens, could this happen?" And Elisha had said, "Behold, you will see it with your own eyes, but you shall not eat of it."«
7.20: And that is what happened to him: the people trampled him underfoot at the gate and he died.

8.1: Elisha spoke to the woman whose child he had restored to life, saying: «Get up, go away, you and your family, and stay wherever you can, for the Lord has called for a famine, and it will indeed come upon the land for seven years.»
8.2: The woman got up and did according to the word of the man of God: she and her family went away and stayed seven years in the land of the Philistines.
8.3: At the end of the seven years, the woman returned from the land of the Philistines and went to plead with the king about her house and her field.
8.4: The king spoke with Giezhi, the servant of the man of God, saying, "Please tell me all the great things that Elisha has done."«
8.5: While the servant was telling the king how his master had raised a dead man from the dead, the woman whose son Elisha had brought back to life began to plead with the king about her house and her field. And Giezi said, «My lord the king, here is the woman and her son whom Elisha brought back to life.»
8.6: The king questioned the woman, who told him the story, and the king appointed a eunuch for her, saying, "Restore everything that belongs to her, with all the income from the field, from the day she left the country until now."«
8.7: Elisha went to Damascus. Ben-Hadad, king of Syria, was sick, and he was informed, saying, "The man of God has arrived here."«
8.8: The king said to Hazael, «Take a gift with you and go to meet the man of God, and inquire of the Lord through him, saying, »Shall I survive this illness?’”
8.9: Hazael went to meet Elisha. He had brought with him a gift, the best of all that was in Damascus, a load of forty camels. When he arrived, he stood before Elisha and said, «Your son Ben-Hadad, king of Syria, has sent me to you to ask, »Will I survive this illness?’”
8.10: Elisha answered him, «Go, tell him: Surely you will survive. But the Lord has shown me that he will surely die.»
8.11: The man of God fixed his gaze on Hazael and stared at him until he blushed, and the man of God wept.
8.12: Hazael said, «Why is my lord weeping?» And Elisha answered, «Because I know the evil you will do to the children of Israel: you will burn their fortified cities, you will kill their young men with the sword, you will dash their little ones to pieces, and you will rip open their pregnant women.»
8.13: Hazael said, "But what is your servant, a dog, that he should do such great things?" Elisha replied, "The Lord has shown me that you will be king of Syria."«
8.14: Hazael, having left Elisha, returned to his master, who said to him, "What did Elisha say to you?" He replied, "He told me: Surely you will survive."«
8.15: The next day, Hazael took a blanket and, having soaked it in water, he spread it over the king's face, and he died. And Hazael reigned in his place.
8.16: In the fifth year of Joram son of Ahab, king of Israel, while Jehoshaphat was still king of Judah, Joram son of Jehoshaphat, king of Judah, began to reign.
8.17: He was thirty-two years old when he became king and he reigned for eight years in Jerusalem.
8.18: He walked in the way of the kings of Israel, as the house of Ahab had done, for he had a daughter of Ahab as his wife, and he did what was evil in the sight of the Lord.
8.19: But the Lord did not want to destroy Judah, for the sake of David his servant, according to the promise he had made to him to always give him a lamp among his sons.
8.20: In his time, Edom freed itself from the domination of Judah and gave itself a king.
8.21: Joram passed through Seira with all the chariots, having risen by night, he defeated the Edomites who surrounded him and the chariot commanders, and the people fled to their tents.
8.22: Edom broke free from Judah's rule, a freedom that persists to this day. Lobna also broke free at the same time.
8.23: The rest of the acts of Joram and all that he did, are they not written in the Book of the Chronicles of Judah?
8.24: Joram rested with his fathers and was buried with his fathers in the city of David, and Ahaziah his son reigned in his place.
8.25: In the twelfth year of Joram son of Ahab, king of Israel, Ahaziah son of Joram, king of Judah, began to reign.
8.26: Ahaziah was twenty-two years old when he became king and he reigned for one year in Jerusalem. His mother was named Athaliah, daughter of Amri, king of Israel.
8.27: He walked in the way of the house of Ahab and did what was evil in the sight of the Lord, like the house of Ahab, for he was a son-in-law of the house of Ahab.
8.28: He went with Joram, son of Ahab, to fight Hazael, king of Syria, at Ramoth-gilead. The Syrians wounded Joram,
8.29: King Joram returned to Jezrehel to recover from the wounds the Syrians had inflicted on him at Ramoth, when he fought against Hazael, king of Syria. Ahaziah, son of Joram, king of Judah, went down to Jezrehel to see Joram, son of Ahab, because he was ill.

9.1: Elisha the prophet called one of the sons of the prophets and said to him, «Gird up your loins, take this flask of oil in your hand and go to Ramoth-in-Gilead.
9.2: When you arrive there, look for Jehu, son of Jehoshaphat, son of Nimshi, and when you have approached him, you will make him rise from among his brothers and lead him into a secluded room.
9.3: You will take the flask of oil, pour it on his head, and say: 'Thus says the Lord: I anoint you king over Israel.' Then you will open the door and flee without delay.»
9.4: The young man, a servant of the prophet, set out for Ramoth-in-Gilead.
9.5: When he arrived, behold, the army commanders were seated. He said, "Commander, I have a word to say to you." And Jehu said, "To which of us?" He answered, "To you, commander."«
9.6: Jehu got up and went into the house and the young man poured the oil on his head, saying to him, «Thus says the Lord, God of Israel: I anoint you king of the Lord’s people, Israel.
9.7: You will strike the house of Ahab, your master, and I will avenge on Jezebel the blood of my servants the prophets and the blood of all the servants of the Lord.
9.8: The entire house of Ahab will perish; I will exterminate every male belonging to Ahab, both slave and free in Israel.
9.9: and I will make the house of Ahab like the house of Jeroboam, son of Nebat, and like the house of Baasha, son of Ahijah.
9.10: The dogs will eat Jezebel in the field of Jezreel, and there will be no one to bury her.» And the young man opened the door and ran away.
9.11: Then Jehu went out to his master's servants. They said to him, "Is everything all right? Why has this madman come to you?" He replied, "You know the man and his language."«
9.12: They replied, "That's a lie. Tell us." And he said, "He spoke to me in such and such a way, saying: 'Thus says the Lord: I anoint you king of Israel.'"«
9.13: Immediately, each man took his cloak and placed it under his feet at the top of the steps. They sounded the trumpet and declared, «Jehu is king.»
9.14: Jehu, son of Jehoshaphat, son of Nimshi, conspired against Joram. Joram and all Israel were then defending Ramoth-gilead against Hazael, king of Syria.,
9.15: But King Joram had returned to Jezrehel to recover from the wounds the Syrians had inflicted on him when he fought against Hazael, king of Syria. Jehu said, «If that is your wish, let no one leave the city to go and tell Jezrehel.»
9.16: And Jehu, having mounted his chariot, set out for Jezrehel, for Joram was lying there and Ahaziah, king of Judah, had gone down to visit Joram.
9.17: The watchman who stood on the tower at Jezreel saw Jehu's troop coming and said, "I see a troop." And Joram said, "Take a horseman and send him to meet them and ask, 'Is it peace?'"«
9.18: The rider went to meet Jehu and said, "This is what the king says: Is it peace?" And Jehu replied, "What do you care about peace? Go behind me." The watchman gave his opinion, saying, "The messenger went to them and he is not returning."«
9.19: Joram sent a second horseman, who came to them and said, "This is what the king says: Is it peace?" And Jehu replied, "What do you care about peace? Go behind me."«
9.20: The watchman gave notice, saying, «The messenger went to them and has not returned. And his conduct is like the conduct of Jehu son of Nimshi, for he drives foolishly.»
9.21: Then Joram said, "Harness," and his chariot was harnessed. Joram, king of Israel, and Ahaziah, king of Judah, each went out in his chariot, and they went out to meet Jehu and met him in the field of Naboth the Jezreelite.
9.22: When Joram saw Jehu, he said to him, "Is this peace, Jehu?" Jehu replied, "What peace is there, as long as the prostitution of Jezebel, your mother, and her many sorceries continue?"«
9.23: Joram turned his horse around and fled, saying to Ochozias, "Treason, Ochozias!"«
9.24: But Jehu took hold of his bow and struck Joram between the shoulders: the arrow came out through his heart and Joram fell down in his chariot.
9.25: And Jehu said to his officer Badacer, «Take him and throw him into the field of Naboth the Jezreelite. For remember: when you and I rode together behind Ahab his father, the Lord pronounced this sentence against him:
9.26: «As surely as I saw yesterday the blood of Naboth and the blood of his sons, declares the Lord, I will repay you in this very field, declares the Lord. So take him and throw him into the field, according to the word of the Lord.»
9.27: Ahaziah, king of Judah, seeing this, fled by way of the garden house. Jehu pursued him and said, «Strike him also, on the chariot.» So they struck him down at the ascent of Gaver, near Jeblaam. He fled to Mageddo and died there.
9.28: His servants transported him in a chariot to Jerusalem and buried him in his tomb with his ancestors in the city of David.
9.29: Ahaziah became king of Judah in the eleventh year of Joram, son of Ahab.
9.30: Jehu entered Jezreel. When Jezebel heard about it, she put on makeup, adorned her head, and looked out of the window.
9.31: As Jehu crossed the threshold, she said, "Is this peace, Zamri, murderer of his master?"«
9.32: He looked up at the window and said, "Who is with me? Who?" And two or three eunuchs looked towards him,
9.33: He said, "Throw her down." They threw her down, and some of her blood splashed on the wall and on the horses, and Jehu trampled her underfoot.
9.34: Then he went in, and after eating and drinking, he said, "Go and see this cursed woman and bury her, for she is a king's daughter."«
9.35: They went to bury her, but they found nothing of her but her skull, her feet, and the palms of her hands.
9.36: They returned and told Jehu, who said, «This is the word of the Lord, which he spoke by his servant Elijah the Tishbite, saying: ‘In the field of Jezreel, dogs will eat the flesh of Jezebel,’,
9.37: And the body of Jezebel will be like dung on the surface of the field, in the field of Jezreel, so that no one will be able to say, »This is Jezebel.”

10.1: There were seventy sons of Ahab in Samaria. Jehu wrote a letter and sent it to the leaders of Jezreel, the elders, and the governors of Ahab's sons in Samaria, saying:
10.2: «As soon as this letter reaches you, since you have with you your master’s sons, with you the chariots and horses, a fortified city and weapons,
10.3: "See which of your master's sons is the best and most suitable; put him on his father's throne and fight for your master's house."»
10.4: They were terrified and said, "Look, the two kings could not resist him, how can we resist?"«
10.5: And the prefect of the palace, the commander of the city, the elders, and the governors sent word to Jehu: «We are your servants and we will do all that you tell us; we will not appoint anyone king, do what seems good to you.»
10.6: Jehu wrote them a second letter, saying, «If you are on my side and will listen to my voice, take the heads of the men, sons of your master, and come to me tomorrow at this time in Jezreel.» Now the seventy sons of the king were with the nobles of the city, who were raising them.
10.7: When the letter reached them, they took the king's sons and slaughtered these seventy men, then, having put their heads in baskets, they sent them to Jehu, to Jezrehel.
10.8: The messenger came and informed him, saying, "They have brought the heads of the king's sons." And he said, "Put them in two piles at the entrance of the gate until morning."«
10.9: In the morning he went out and, presenting himself to all the people, said: «You are righteous: behold, I conspired against my master and killed him, but who struck down all these?
10.10: Therefore, know that nothing of the word of the Lord, the word that the Lord spoke concerning the house of Ahab, will fall to the ground; the Lord has fulfilled what he declared through his servant Elijah.»
10.11: And Jehu struck down all who remained of the house of Ahab in Jezrehel, all his nobles, his close associates, and his priests, until not one of them remained.
10.12: Then, having gotten up, he set off for Samaria. Arriving at a shepherds' meeting house on the road,
10.13: Jehu found the brothers of Ahaziah, king of Judah, and said, «Who are you?» They replied, «We are the brothers of Ahaziah, and we have come down to greet the sons of the king and the sons of the queen.»
10.14: Jehu said, "Seize them alive." And having seized them alive, they slaughtered them at the cistern of the house of meeting, forty-two of them, without Jehu letting a single one escape.
10.15: Leaving that place, he met Jonadab son of Rechab, who was coming to meet him. He greeted him and said, «Is your heart as sincere as mine is toward yours?» Jonadab replied, «It is.» Jehu said, «If it is, give me your hand.» He gave him his hand, and Jehu had him ride with him in the chariot.,
10.16: saying, "Come with me and you will see my zeal for the Lord." So he took him with him in his chariot.
10.17: Arriving in Samaria, Jehu struck down all those who remained in Samaria of the family of Ahab and he exterminated them, according to the word that the Lord had spoken to Elijah.
10.18: Jehu then gathered all the people together and said to them, «Ahab served Baal a little, but Jehu will serve him much.”.
10.19: »Now summon to me all the prophets of Baal, all his servants, and all his priests—not one of them—for I have a great sacrifice to offer to Baal; whoever is missing shall not live.” But Jehu was acting cunningly to destroy the servants of Baal.
10.20: Jehu said, "Proclaim a solemn assembly in honor of Baal," and they proclaimed it.
10.21: Jehu sent messengers throughout Israel and all the servants of Baal came, not one of them was left out; they entered the house of Baal and the house of Baal was filled from one end to the other.
10.22: Jehu said to the one who was in charge of the wardrobe, "Bring out clothes for all the servants of Baal." And the man brought out clothes for them.
10.23: Then Jehu, having come to the house of Baal with Jonadab son of Rechab, said to the servants of Baal, «Search and see that there are none of the servants of the Lord here with us, but only servants of Baal.»
10.24: When they had gone in to offer sacrifices and burnt offerings, Jehu posted eighty men outside, saying to them, «If any of the men I put in your hands escapes, the life of the one who let him escape will pay for his own.»
10.25: As soon as he had finished offering the burnt offering, Jehu said to the guards and officers, «Go in and strike them down; not one of them should leave.» So they struck them with the edge of the sword. The guards and officers threw them down there and, entering the sanctuary of the house of Baal,
10.26: They took out the pillars of the house of Baal and burned them,
10.27: They smashed the Baal stele to pieces, they also demolished the house of Baal and made it a cesspool, which has survived to this day.
10.28: Jehu exterminated Baal from the midst of Israel.
10.29: But Jehu did not turn away from the sins of Jeroboam, son of Nebat, who had caused Israel to sin, nor from the golden calves that were at Bethel and that were at Dan.
10.30: The Lord said to Jehu, «Because you have done well what was right in my eyes and have done to the house of Ahab all that was in my heart, your sons to the fourth generation shall sit on the throne of Israel.»
10.31: But Jehu did not take care to walk with all his heart in the law of the Lord, the God of Israel; he did not turn away from the sins of Jeroboam who had caused Israel to sin.
10.32: In those days, the Lord began to cut off the territory of Israel, and Hazael defeated the Israelites throughout the entire border of Israel.,
10.33: From the Jordan, towards the rising sun, he defeated all the land of Gilead, the Gadites, the Reubenites, the Manassites, from Aroer, which is on the Arnon River, to Gilead and Bashan.
10.34: The rest of the acts of Jehu, all that he did and all his exploits, are they not written in the book of the Chronicles of the kings of Israel?
10.35: And Jehu rested with his ancestors and was buried in Samaria. Jehoahaz, his son, reigned in his place.
10.36: The time that Jehu reigned over Israel in Samaria was twenty-eight years.

11.1: Athaliah, mother of Ahaziah, seeing that her son was dead, rose up and caused the entire royal line to perish.
11.2: But Josaba, daughter of King Joram and sister of Ahaziah, took Joash, son of Ahaziah, and rescued him from among the king's sons who were being slaughtered. She put him and his nurse in the bedroom. Thus he was hidden from Athaliah's sight, and he was not put to death.
11.3: He remained hidden with Joshua in the house of the Lord for six years, and Athaliah reigned over the land.
11.4: In the seventh year, Jehoiada sent for the centurions of the Carians and the guards and brought them to him in the house of the Lord. He made a covenant with them and, after they had sworn an oath in the house of the Lord, he showed them the king's son.
11.5: Then he gave them his orders, saying, «This is what you are to do: A third of you who come on duty on the Sabbath day to stand guard at the king’s house,
11.6: one third at the gate of Sur and one third at the gate of the guards, you shall guard the house of the Lord to prevent anyone from entering.
11.7: And your two other divisions, all those who go off duty on the Sabbath day to stand guard at the house of the Lord before the king,
11.8: You will surround the king on all sides, each with weapons in hand; if anyone enters the ranks, he must be put to death, and you will be near the king when he goes out and when he comes in.»
11.9: The centurions acted according to all that Jehoiada the priest had commanded. Each took their men, those who came on duty on the Sabbath day and those who came off duty on the Sabbath day, and went to Jehoiada the priest.
11.10: The priest handed over to the centurions the spears and shields that had belonged to King David and that were in the house of the Lord.
11.11: The guards, each with weapons in hand, stood from the right side of the house to the left side of the house, near the altar and near the house, so as to surround the king.
11.12: And the priest brought forward the king's son and placed the diadem and the testimony on him. They made him king and anointed him, and clapping their hands, they said, "Long live the king!"«
11.13: When Athaliah heard the noise of the guards and the people, she came to the people at the house of the Lord.
11.14: She looked, and behold, the king was standing on the platform, as was the custom. Near the king were the chiefs and the trumpeters, and all the people of the land were rejoicing, and the trumpets were blowing. Athaliah tore her clothes and cried out, «Conspiracy! Conspiracy!»
11.15: Then Jehoiada the priest gave an order to the centurions who were in command of the army, saying, «Bring her out of the house between the ranks and kill with the sword anyone who follows her.» For the priest had said, «She must not be put to death in the house of the Lord.»
11.16: She was given space on both sides and she went by the way of the entrance to the horses, towards the king's house and it was there that she was killed.
11.17: Jehoiada made a covenant between the Lord, the king and the people, by which they were to be the Lord's people; he also made a covenant between the king and the people.
11.18: And all the people of the land went into the house of Baal and demolished it, smashing its altars and images to pieces, and killing Mathan, the priest of Baal, before the altars. After placing guards in the house of the Lord,
11.19: The priest Jehoiada took the centurions, the Carians, the guards, and all the people of the land, and they brought the king down from the house of the Lord, and they entered the king's house through the gate of the guards, and Joash sat on the throne of the kings.
11.20: All the people of the country rejoiced and the city was at peace, and Athaliah was put to death by the sword in the king's house.

12.1: Joash was seven years old when he became king.
12.2: In the seventh year of Jehu, Joash became king and reigned forty years in Jerusalem. His mother's name was Shebiah, from Beersheba.
12.3: Joash did what was right in the eyes of the Lord as long as Jehoiada the priest gave him instructions.
12.4: However, the sacred places did not disappear; the people continued to offer sacrifices and perfumes at the sacred sites.
12.5: Joash said to the priests, «All the money from the sanctuary that is brought into the house of the Lord, namely, the money from the personal tax, the money for the redemption of persons according to each person’s valuation, and all the money that each person has it in their heart to bring to the house of the Lord,
12.6: "Let the priests take it, each one of the people he knows, and let them repair the breaches in the house, wherever they can find it."»
12.7: Now it came to pass that, in the twenty-third year of King Joash, the priests had not repaired the breaches in the house.
12.8: King Joash summoned Jehoiada the priest and the other priests and said to them, «Why don’t you repair the breaches in the house? Now you will no longer take money from your acquaintances, but you will give it up to repair the breaches in the house.»
12.9: The priests agreed to stop taking money from the people and they no longer had to repair the breaches in the house.
12.10: Then Jehoiada the priest took a chest and, having made a hole in its lid, he placed it beside the altar, to the right of the passage through which one entered the house of the Lord; the priests who guarded the gate put into it all the money that was brought into the house of the Lord.
12.11: When they saw that there was a lot of money in the chest, the king's secretary would go up with the high priest and they would bind and count the money that was in the house of the Lord.
12.12: They handed over the weighed money to those in charge of the Lord's house who were carrying out the work, and these men gave it to the carpenters and other workers who were working on the Lord's house.,
12.13: to the masons and stonecutters, they also gave it for the purchase of wood and dressed stone needed to repair the breaches in the Lord's house and for everything that was spent to consolidate the house.
12.14: However, no silver basins, knives, bowls, trumpets, gold utensils, or silver utensils were made for the house of the Lord with the silver that was brought into the house of the Lord:
12.15: It was given to those who were carrying out the work, so that they could use it to repair the house of the Lord.
12.16: No accounting was required of the men into whose hands the money was handed to those who carried out the work, because they behaved with integrity.
12.17: The money for the guilt offerings and the sin offerings was not brought into the house of the Lord; it belonged to the priests.
12.18: Then Hazael, king of Syria, went up and fought against Geth, which he captured. He resolved to go up against Jerusalem.
12.19: Joash, king of Judah, took all the consecrated things, which Jehoshaphat, Joram and Ahaziah, his fathers, kings of Judah, had consecrated, which he himself had consecrated, and all the gold which was found in the treasuries of the house of the Lord and of the house of the king, and he sent all of it to Hazael, king of Syria, who departed from Jerusalem.
12.20: The rest of the acts of Joash and all that he did, are they not written in the book of the Chronicles of the Kings of Judah?
12.21: His servants rose up and, having formed a conspiracy, they struck down Joash in the house of Mello, on the descent from Sella.
12.22: It was Jehoshara son of Shemaath and Jehosabad son of Somer, his servants, who struck him down, and he died. He was buried with his ancestors in the city of David, and Amaziah his son reigned in his place.

13.1: In the twenty-third year of Joash son of Ahaziah, king of Judah, Jehoahaz son of Jehu reigned over Israel in Samaria; he reigned seventeen years.
13.2: He did what was evil in the sight of the Lord; he imitated the sins of Jeroboam, son of Nebat, who had caused Israel to sin, and he did not turn away from them.
13.3: The Lord’s anger was kindled against Israel, and he delivered them into the hands of Hazael king of Syria and into the hands of Benhadad son of Hazael all the time.
13.4: Jehoahaz implored the Lord and the Lord heard him, for he saw the anguish of Israel, oppressed by the king of Syria.
13.5: And the Lord gave Israel a deliverer; rescued from the power of the Syrians, the children of Israel lived in their tents as before.
13.6: But they did not turn away from the sins of the house of Jeroboam, which had caused Israel to sin; they walked in them, and even the Asherah remained standing in Samaria.
13.7: For the Lord left Jehoahaz no other armed people except fifty horsemen, ten chariots and ten thousand foot soldiers, for the king of Syria had destroyed them and made them like ashes that are trampled underfoot.
13.8: The rest of the acts of Jehoahaz, all that he did and his exploits, are they not written in the book of the Chronicles of the Kings of Israel?
13.9: Joahaz rested with his ancestors and was buried in Samaria, and Joash his son reigned in his place.
13.10: In the thirty-seventh year of Joash king of Judah, Joash son of Jehoahaz reigned over Israel in Samaria, he reigned sixteen years.
13.11: He did what was evil in the sight of the Lord; he did not turn away from any of the sins of Jeroboam, son of Nebat, who had caused Israel to sin; he walked in them.
13.12: The rest of the acts of Joash, all that he did, his exploits, and how he fought with Amaziah, are they not written in the book of the Chronicles of the Kings of Israel?
13.13: Joash rested with his fathers and Jeroboam sat on his throne and Joash was buried in Samaria with the kings of Israel.
13.14: Elisha was suffering from the illness from which he died. Joash, king of Israel, went down to him and wept over his face, saying, «My father! My father! Chariot of Israel and its horsemen!»
13.15: Elisha said to him, "Take a bow and arrows." So he took a bow and arrows.
13.16: And Elisha said to the king of Israel, «Put your hand on the bow.» When he had put his hand on the bow, Elisha placed his hands on the king’s hands.,
13.17: And he said, «Open the window facing east,» and he opened it. Elisha said, «Fly an arrow,» and he flew an arrow. Elisha said, «This is the Lord’s arrow of deliverance, the arrow of deliverance against the Syrians. You shall strike down the Syrians at Aphek until they are destroyed.»
13.18: Elisha said again, "Take the arrows." And he took them. Elisha said to the king of Israel, "Strike the ground." He struck the ground three times and stopped.
13.19: The man of God became angry with him and said, "You should have struck the ground five or six times, then you would have beaten the Syrians to their extermination, but now you will beat the Syrians three times."«
13.20: Elisha died and was buried. The Moabite bands entered the country when the new year came around.
13.21: As they were burying a man, one of the bands of cloth was seen, and they threw the man into Elisha's tomb. The man touched Elisha's bones, and he came to life and stood up on his feet.
13.22: Hazael, king of Syria, had oppressed Israel throughout the life of Jehoahaz.
13.23: But the Lord had mercy on them and compassion on them; he turned to them because of his covenant with Abraham, Isaac, and Jacob; he did not want to destroy them, nor has he rejected them from his presence to this day.
13.24: Hazael, king of Syria, died and Benhadad, his son, reigned in his place.
13.25: Joash, son of Jehoahaz, recaptured from Ben-Hadad, son of Hazael, the cities that Hazael had taken from his father Jehoahaz in the war. Joash defeated him three times and recovered the cities of Israel.

14.1: In the second year of Joash son of Jehoahaz, king of Israel, Amaziah son of Joash, king of Judah, began to reign.
14.2: He was twenty-five years old when he became king and he reigned twenty-nine years in Jerusalem. His mother's name was Jehoadan, from Jerusalem.
14.3: He did what was right in the eyes of the Lord, but not like David his father; he did everything like Joash his father.
14.4: However, the sacred places did not disappear; the people continued to offer sacrifices and perfumes at the sacred sites.
14.5: When the kingship was firmly established in his hand, he struck down his servants, who had killed the king, his father.
14.6: But he did not put the sons of the murderers to death, according to what is written in the book of the law of Moses, where the Lord gives this commandment: «Fathers shall not be put to death for their children, nor children put to death for their fathers, but each shall die for their own sin.»
14.7: He defeated ten thousand Edomites in the Valley of Salt and he stormed Sela and gave it the name Jectehel, which it has retained to this day.
14.8: Then Amaziah sent messengers to Joash, son of Jehoahaz, son of Jehu, king of Israel, saying, «Come, let us meet face to face.»
14.9: And Joash, king of Israel, sent word to Amaziah, king of Judah: «The thornbush in Lebanon sent word to the cedarbush in Lebanon: ‘Give your daughter as a wife to my son.’ And the wild beasts in Lebanon passed over and trampled the thornbush underfoot.”.
14.10: You have truly defeated the Edomites, and your heart has been lifted up. Glory yourself and remain at home. Why go about your business in disaster, so that you and Judah may fall with you?»
14.11: But Amaziah did not listen to him. And Joash, king of Israel, went up and they met face to face, he and Amaziah, king of Judah, at Bethsames, which is in Judah.
14.12: Judah was defeated before Israel, and everyone fled to their tents.
14.13: Joash, king of Israel, took Amaziah, king of Judah, son of Joash, son of Ahaziah, from Bethsames. He came to Jerusalem and made a breach of four hundred cubits in the wall of Jerusalem, from the Ephraim Gate to the Corner Gate.
14.14: He took all the gold and silver and all the utensils that were in the house of the Lord and in the treasuries of the house of the king, he also took hostages and returned to Samaria.
14.15: The rest of the acts of Joash, what he did and how he fought with Amaziah, king of Judah, are they not written in the book of the Chronicles of the kings of Israel?
14.16: Joash rested with his ancestors and was buried in Samaria with the kings of Israel. Jeroboam, his son, reigned in his place.
14.17: Amaziah, son of Joash, king of Judah, lived fifteen years after the death of Joash, son of Jehoahaz, king of Israel.
14.18: Are not the rest of Amaziah's deeds written in the book of the Chronicles of the Kings of Judah?
14.19: A conspiracy was hatched against him in Jerusalem and he fled to Lachish, but men were sent after him to Lachish and he was put to death there.
14.20: He was transported on horses and buried in Jerusalem with his ancestors, in the city of David.
14.21: And all the people of Judah took Azariah, who was sixteen years old, and made him king in place of his father Amaziah.
14.22: Azariah rebuilt Elath and brought it back to Judah, after the king had rested with his ancestors.
14.23: In the fifteenth year of Amaziah son of Joash, king of Judah, Jeroboam son of Joash king of Israel reigned in Samaria, his reign was forty-one years.
14.24: He did what was evil in the sight of the Lord; he did not turn away from any of the sins of Jeroboam, son of Nebat, who had caused Israel to sin.
14.25: He restored the boundaries of Israel from the entrance of Emath to the Sea of Arabah, according to the word of the Lord, the God of Israel, which he had spoken by the mouth of his servant Jonah the prophet, the son of Amathi, who was from Geth-Shepher.
14.26: For the Lord saw the very bitter affliction of Israel, where there was no married man, no free man, nor anyone to come to the aid of Israel.
14.27: And the Lord had not yet resolved to blot out the name of Israel from under heaven, but he delivered them by the hand of Jeroboam, son of Joash.
14.28: The rest of the acts of Jeroboam, all that he did, his exploits, how he fought and how he brought back to Israel Damascus and Emath which had belonged to Judah, is this not written in the book of the Chronicles of the kings of Israel?
14.29: Jeroboam rested with his fathers, with the kings of Israel, and Zechariah, his son, reigned in his place.

15.1: In the twenty-seventh year of Jeroboam king of Israel, Azariah son of Amaziah king of Judah began to reign.
15.2: He was sixteen years old when he became king and he reigned fifty-two years in Jerusalem. His mother's name was Jechelia, from Jerusalem.
15.3: He did what was right in the eyes of the Lord, according to all that Amaziah his father had done.
15.4: However, the sacred places did not disappear; the people continued to offer sacrifices and perfumes at the sacred sites.
15.5: The Lord struck the king, who became a leper until the day of his death and lived in a secluded house. Jotham, the king's son, was in charge of the house, judging the people of the land.
15.6: The rest of the acts of Azariah and all that he did, are they not written in the book of the Chronicles of the Kings of Judah?
15.7: Azarias rested with his ancestors and was buried with them in the city of David, and Jotham his son reigned in his place.
15.8: In the thirty-eighth year of Azariah king of Judah, Zechariah son of Jeroboam reigned over Israel in Samaria; his reign was six months.
15.9: He did what was evil in the sight of the Lord, as his fathers had done; he did not turn away from the sins of Jeroboam, son of Nebat, who had caused Israel to sin.
15.10: Sellum, son of Jabez, conspired against him and, having struck him down before the people, he put him to death and reigned in his place.
15.11: The rest of the acts of Zechariah are written in the book of the Chronicles of the Kings of Israel.
15.12: This was the word of the Lord, which he had spoken to Jehu: «Your sons to the fourth generation shall sit on the throne of Israel.» And it was so.
15.13: Shallum, son of Jabez, became king in the thirty-ninth year of Uzziah, king of Judah, and he reigned for one month in Samaria.
15.14: Manahem, son of Gadi, went up from Terah and came to Samaria, and struck down Shellum, son of Jabesh, in Samaria and killed him and reigned in his place.
15.15: The rest of the acts of Sellum and the conspiracy he hatched, behold, this is written in the book of the Chronicles of the kings of Israel.
15.16: Then Manahem, having departed from Terah, struck Thapsah and all who were there, with its territory, he struck it, because it had not opened its gates, and he ripped open the bellies of all the pregnant women.
15.17: In the thirty-ninth year of Azariah king of Judah, Manahem son of Gadi became king over Israel, and he reigned ten years in Samaria.
15.18: He did what was evil in the sight of the Lord; he did not turn away, as long as he lived, from the sins of Jeroboam, son of Nebat, who had caused Israel to sin.
15.19: Phul, king of Assyria, came to the country and Manahem gave Phul a thousand talents of silver so that he would come to his aid and strengthen the kingdom in his hand.
15.20: Manahem levied this money on Israel, on all those who had great wealth, to give to the king of Assyria; he demanded fifty shekels of silver from each one. The king of Assyria returned and did not stay there in the land.
15.21: The rest of the acts of Manahem and all that he did, are they not written in the book of the Chronicles of the Kings of Israel?
15.22: Manahem rested with his fathers, and Phaceiah, his son, reigned in his place.
15.23: In the fiftieth year of Azariah king of Judah, Phaceiah son of Manahem reigned over Israel in Samaria; his reign was two years.
15.24: He did what was evil in the sight of the Lord; he did not turn away from the sins of Jeroboam, son of Nebat, who had caused Israel to sin.
15.25: Phacaeus, son of Romelias, his officer, conspired against him. He struck him down in Samaria in the tower of the king's house, along with Argob and Arie. He had with him fifty men from among the sons of the Gileadites. He put Phaceiah to death and reigned in his place.
15.26: The rest of the acts of Phaceiah and all that he did, behold, this is written in the book of the Chronicles of the kings of Israel.
15.27: In the fifty-second year of Azariah king of Judah, Phacah son of Romaliah reigned over Israel in Samaria, and his reign lasted twenty years.
15.28: He did what was evil in the sight of the Lord; he did not turn away from the sins of Jeroboam, son of Nebat, who had caused Israel to sin.
15.29: In the days of Phacaeus king of Israel, Tiglath-pileser king of Assyria came and took Aijon, Abel-Beth-Machaah, Janoah, Kedesh, Ashor, Gilead and Galilee, all the land of Naphtali, and he carried the inhabitants captive to Assyria.
15.30: Hosea, son of Elah, having formed a conspiracy against Phacaeus, son of Romelias, struck him down and killed him, and then reigned in his place, in the twentieth year of Jotham, son of Uzziah.
15.31: The rest of the acts of Phacaeus and all that he did, behold, it is written in the book of the Chronicles of the kings of Israel.
15.32: In the second year of Phacaeus, son of Romélias, king of Israel, Jotham, son of Uzziah, king of Judah, reigned.
15.33: He was twenty-five years old when he became king and he reigned sixteen years in Jerusalem. His mother's name was Jerusalem, daughter of Zadok.
15.34: He did what was right in the eyes of the Lord, he acted entirely as his father Uzziah had acted.
15.35: However, the sacred places did not disappear; the people continued to offer sacrifices and incense at the sacred sites. Joatham built the upper gate of the house of the Lord.
15.36: The rest of the acts of Jotham and all that he did, are they not written in the book of the Chronicles of the Kings of Judah?
15.37: At that time the Lord began to send against Judah: Rezin, king of Syria, and Phacaeus, son of Romelias.
15.38: Joatham rested with his ancestors and was buried with them in the city of David, his father. Ahaz, his son, reigned in his place.

16.1: In the seventeenth year of Phacaeus son of Romélias, Ahaz son of Jotham reigned, king of Judah.
16.2: Ahaz was twenty years old when he became king, and he reigned sixteen years in Jerusalem. He did not do what was right in the eyes of the Lord his God, as his father David had done.
16.3: But he walked in the way of the kings of Israel and even made his son pass through the fire, according to the abominations of the nations that the Lord had driven out before the children of Israel.
16.4: He offered sacrifices and perfumes at sacred places, on the hills and under every green tree.
16.5: Then Rezin, king of Syria, and Phacaeus, son of Romélias, king of Israel, went up against Jerusalem to attack it. They besieged Ahaz, but they could not defeat him.
16.6: At that same time, Rasin, king of Syria, brought Elath back into the power of the Syrians, he expelled the Jews from Elath and the Syrians came to Elath, where they have lived to this day.
16.7: Ahaz sent messengers to Tiglath-pileser, king of Assyria, saying, «I am your servant and your son; come up and deliver me from the hand of the king of Syria and from the hand of the king of Israel, who have risen up against me.»
16.8: And Ahaz took the silver and gold that was found in the house of the Lord and in the treasuries of the king's house and sent it as a present to the king of Assyria.
16.9: The king of Assyria listened to him and the king of Assyria marched against Damascus and, having taken it, he took its inhabitants captive to Qir and he put Rasin to death.
16.10: King Ahaz went to Damascus to meet Tiglath-pileser, king of Assyria. Having seen the altar that was in Damascus, King Ahaz sent to the priest Uriah the model of the altar and its form according to all his work.
16.11: The priest Urias built the altar, the priest Urias made it entirely according to the model that King Ahaz had sent from Damascus, before the king returned from Damascus.
16.12: Upon his arrival from Damascus, the king saw the altar. The king approached the altar and climbed onto it.,
16.13: He burned his burnt offering and his grain offering, poured out his libation, and sprinkled on the altar the blood of his peace offerings.
16.14: He removed the bronze altar that was before the Lord from in front of the house, from between the new altar and the house of the Lord, and placed it beside the new altar, towards the north.
16.15: King Ahaz also gave this order to the priest Uriah: «Burn on the great altar the morning burnt offering and the evening grain offering, the king’s burnt offering and his grain offering, the burnt offering of all the people of the land and their grain offering, pour out their drink offering on it, and sprinkle on it all the blood of the burnt offerings and all the blood of the sacrifices. As for the bronze altar, it is my responsibility to provide for it.»
16.16: The priest Urias did everything that King Ahaz had ordered.
16.17: Furthermore, King Ahaz broke down the frames and bases and removed the basins that were on them; he lowered the bronze sea from the bronze oxen that supported it and placed it on a stone pavement.,
16.18: He changed, in the house of the Lord, in consideration for the king of Assyria, the Sabbath portico, which had been built in the house and the outer entrance of the king.
16.19: The rest of the acts of Ahaz and all that he did, are they not written in the book of the Chronicles of the Kings of Judah?
16.20: Ahaz rested with his ancestors and was buried with them in the City of David. Hezekiah, his son, reigned in his place.

17.1: In the twelfth year of Ahaz king of Judah, Hoshea son of Elah reigned over Israel in Samaria, and his reign lasted nine years.
17.2: He did what was evil in the sight of the Lord, but not like the kings of Israel who had been before him.
17.3: Shalmaneser, king of Assyria, came up against him and Hoshea was subjugated and paid him tribute.
17.4: But the king of Assyria discovered a conspiracy by Hoshea, who had sent messengers to Sua, king of Egypt, and who was no longer paying tribute to the king of Assyria, year after year, so the king of Assyria had him seized and thrown in chains into a prison.
17.5: And the king of Assyria traveled throughout the land and came up against Samaria, besieging it for three years.
17.6: In the ninth year of Hosea, the king of Assyria captured Samaria and carried Israel captive to Assyria. He assigned them Halah, the banks of the Habor, the river of Gosan, and the cities of the Medes as their dwelling place.
17.7: This happened because the children of Israel had sinned against the Lord their God, who had brought them up out of the land of Egypt, from under the hand of Pharaoh, king of Egypt, and because they had feared other gods.
17.8: They followed the rites of the nations that the Lord had driven out before the children of Israel and those that the kings of Israel had established.
17.9: The children of Israel disguised under a false appearance things that were not right in the sight of the Lord their God. They built themselves holy places in all their cities, from watchtowers to fortified cities.
17.10: They set up pillars and Asherahs on every high hill and under every green tree.
17.11: And there they burned incense on all the holy places, like the nations that the Lord had taken captive before them, and they did evil things, thus provoking the Lord.
17.12: They served idols, about which the Lord had told them, «You shall not do that.»
17.13: The Lord bore witness against Israel and Judah through all his prophets and seers, saying, «Turn from your evil ways and keep my commandments and my ordinances, following all the law that I commanded your fathers and that I sent to you by my servants the prophets.»
17.14: But they did not listen and they stiffened their necks, like their fathers, who did not believe in the Lord their God.
17.15: They rejected his ordinances and the covenant he had made with their ancestors, and the testimonies he had given against them. They went after worthless things and indulged in vanity, following the nations around them, whom the Lord had commanded them not to imitate.
17.16: They abandoned all the commandments of the Lord their God, they made for themselves two molten calves and they made themselves Asherahs, they bowed down before all the host of heaven and they served Baal.
17.17: They made their sons and daughters pass through the fire, they practiced divination and enchantments, and they devoted themselves to doing evil in the sight of the Lord, provoking him to anger.
17.18: And the Lord was very angry with Israel and removed them from his presence. Only the tribe of Judah remained.,
17.19: although Judah itself had not kept the commandments of the Lord, their God, and had followed the rites established by Israel.
17.20: The Lord rejected all the descendants of Israel, afflicted them, and gave them into the hands of plunderers, until he drove them out of his presence.
17.21: For Israel had broken away from the house of David and they had made Jeroboam, son of Nebat, king, and Jeroboam had turned Israel away from the Lord and caused them to commit a great sin.
17.22: And the children of Israel walked in all the sins that Jeroboam had committed; they did not turn away from them.,
17.23: until the Lord had driven Israel out of his presence, as he had said through all his servants the prophets. And Israel was taken captive from their land to Assyria, where they remain to this day.
17.24: The king of Assyria brought people from Babylon, Cuthah, Avah, Emath, and Sepharvaim and settled them in the cities of Samaria in place of the children of Israel; they took possession of Samaria and lived in its cities.
17.25: When they began to live there, they did not fear the Lord, and the Lord sent lions against them, and they killed them.
17.26: So this report was made to the king of Assyria: «The nations you have deported and settled in the cities of Samaria do not know how to serve the god of the land, and he has sent lions against them, and behold, they are killing them because they do not know how to serve the god of the land.»
17.27: The king of Assyria gave this order: «Send one of the priests you brought from there into captivity, let him go and settle there and teach them how to serve the god of the land.»
17.28: One of the priests who had been taken captive from Samaria came to settle in Bethel and taught them how they should honor the Lord.
17.29: But each nation made its own gods and placed them in the houses of the sacred places built by the Samaritans, each nation in the city it inhabited.
17.30: The people of Babylon made Sochoth-Benoth, the people of Cuthah made Nergel, the people of Emath made Asima,
17.31: Those of Avah made Nebahaz and Tarthach, and those of Shapharvaim gave their children to the fire in honor of Adramelech and Anamelech, gods of Shapharvaim.
17.32: They also honored the Lord and appointed priests of the holy places from among all the people, and these priests offered sacrifices for them in the houses of the holy places.
17.33: Thus they honored the Lord and at the same time served their gods according to the custom of the nations from which they had been deported.
17.34: They follow the old customs to this day; they do not fear the Lord, nor do they conform to their regulations and ordinances, nor to the law and commandments given by the Lord to the children of Jacob, whom he called by the name of Israel.
17.35: The Lord had made a covenant with them and had given them this command: «You shall not fear other gods, you shall not bow down to them, you shall not serve them or offer them sacrifices.
17.36: But the Lord your God, who brought you up out of the land of Egypt with great power and with his outstretched arm, is the one you shall fear, before him you shall bow down, and to him you shall offer sacrifices.
17.37: You shall observe the precepts, the ordinances, the law and the commandments which he has written for you, always putting them into practice, and you shall not fear other gods.
17.38: You will not forget the alliance I have made with you, and you will not fear any other gods.
17.39: But you must fear the Lord your God, and he will deliver you from the hand of all your enemies.»
17.40: And they did not obey, but they followed their original customs.
17.41: Thus these nations feared the Lord and at the same time served their images and their children and their children's children do to this day what their fathers did.

18.1: In the third year of Hoshea son of Elah, king of Israel, Hezekiah son of Ahaz, king of Judah, began to reign.
18.2: He was twenty-five years old when he became king and he reigned twenty-nine years in Jerusalem. His mother's name was Abi, daughter of Zechariah.
18.3: He did what was right in the eyes of the Lord, according to all that David his father had done.
18.4: He removed the sacred places, broke the pillars, cut down the Asherah poles, and smashed the bronze serpent that Moses had made, for the children of Israel had until then burned incense before it: it was called Nohestan.
18.5: He put his trust in the Lord, the God of Israel, and he had no equal among all the kings of Judah who came after him or who preceded him.
18.6: Attached to the Lord, he did not turn away from him and he observed his commandments which the Lord had prescribed to Moses.
18.7: And the Lord was with Hezekiah, and he succeeded in all his undertakings. He rebelled against the king of Assyria and was no longer subject to him.
