18.8: He defeated the Philistines as far as Gaza and ravaged their territory, from the watchtower to the fortified cities.
18.9: In the fourth year of King Hezekiah, which was the seventh year of Hoshea son of Elah, king of Israel, Shalmaneser king of Assyria came up against Samaria and besieged it.
18.10: It was taken after three years, in the sixth year of Hezekiah, which was the ninth of Hoshea king of Israel, that Samaria was taken.
18.11: The king of Assyria took Israel captive to Assyria, and settled them in Halah, on the banks of the Habor, the river of Goshan, and in the cities of the Medes.,
18.12: because they had not listened to the voice of the Lord their God and had transgressed his covenant, because they had neither listened to nor put into practice all that Moses, the servant of the Lord, had commanded.
18.13: In the fourteenth year of King Hezekiah, Sennacherib king of Assyria came up against all the fortified cities of Judah and captured them.
18.14: Hezekiah, king of Judah, sent word to the king of Assyria at Lachish: «I have done wrong; leave me alone. Whatever you impose on me, I will accept.» So the king of Assyria imposed on Hezekiah, king of Judah, three hundred talents of silver and thirty talents of gold.
18.15: Hezekiah gave all the money that was found in the house of the Lord and in the treasuries of the king's house.
18.16: At that time Hezekiah, king of Judah, broke down the gates of the temple of the Lord and the pillars which Hezekiah, king of Judah, had himself overlaid with gold, and he gave the gold to the king of Assyria.
18.17: The king of Assyria sent from Lachish to King Hezekiah his commander-in-chief, his chief of the eunuchs, and his chief cupbearer with a large force. They went up and arrived in Jerusalem. When they had gone up and arrived, they stopped at the aqueduct of the Upper Pool, on the road to the Fuller's Field.,
18.18: and called for the king. Eliacim, son of Helkiah, head of the king's household, went to them, with Shebna the secretary and Joah, son of Asaph, the recorder.
18.19: The chief cupbearer said to them, «Tell Hezekiah: This is what the great king, the king of Assyria, says: What is this confidence you are relying on?
18.20: You spoke with empty words. I have counsel and strength for war. And now, in whom do you trust to rebel against me?
18.21: Behold, now you rely on the support of this broken reed, Egypt, which penetrates and pierces the hand of whoever leans on it: such is Pharaoh, king of Egypt, to all who trust in him.
18.22: Perhaps you will say to me: We trust in the Lord our God. But is this not the one whose holy places and altars Hezekiah removed, saying to Judah and Jerusalem: You shall worship before this altar in Jerusalem?
18.23: Now, make a deal with my master, the king of Assyria: I will give you two thousand horses, if you can provide riders to ride them.
18.24: How could you repel even one of the least of my master's servants? Therefore you put your trust in Egypt for chariots and horsemen.
18.25: Now, was it against the Lord's will that I came up against this place to destroy it? The Lord said to me, "Go up against this land and destroy it."«
18.26: Eliacim son of Helkiah, Shebna, and Joah said to the chief cupbearer, «Speak to your servants in Aramaic, for we understand it, and do not speak to us in the Hebrew language in the hearing of the people who are on the wall.»
18.27: The chief cupbearer replied, "Did my master send me these words to you and your master? Didn't he send them to these men sitting on the wall, to eat their excrement and drink their urine with you?"«
18.28: Then the chief cupbearer stepped forward and shouted aloud in the Jewish language, saying: «Listen to the word of the great king, the king of Assyria.
18.29: Thus says the king: Let Hezekiah not deceive you, for he will not be able to deliver you from his hand.
18.30: Let Hezekiah not persuade you to trust in the Lord, saying, "The Lord will surely deliver us, and this city will not be given into the hands of the king of Assyria.".
18.31: Do not listen to Hezekiah, for thus says the king of Assyria: Make peace with me and surrender to me, and let each of you eat from his own vine and each of you from his own fig tree, and let each of you drink water from his own well,
18.32: until I come and take you to a land like your own, a land of grain and new wine, a land of bread and vineyards, a land of olive trees and honey, so that you may live and not die. Therefore do not listen to Hezekiah, for he is deceiving you when he says, “The Lord will deliver us.”.
18.33: Did the gods of the nations each deliver their country from the hand of the king of Assyria?
18.34: Where are the gods of Emath and Arphad? Where are the gods of Sepharvaim, Anah, and Ava? Have they delivered Samaria from my hand?
18.35: Which of the gods of the lands have delivered their lands from my hand, so that the Lord may deliver Jerusalem from my hand?»
18.36: The people remained silent and did not answer him a word, for the king had given this order: "You shall not answer him."«
18.37: And Eliacim, son of Helkiah, head of the king's household, Shebna, the secretary, and Joah, son of Asaph, the recorder, came to Hezekiah with their clothes torn and reported to him the words of the chief cupbearer.

19.1: When King Hezekiah heard this report, he tore his clothes, covered himself with sackcloth, and went into the house of the Lord.
19.2: He sent Eliacim, the head of his household, Shebna, the secretary, and the elders of the priests, covered in sackcloth, to Isaiah the prophet, son of Amos.
19.3: They told him, "This is what Hezekiah said: This day is a day of distress, rebuke, and disgrace, because the children are about to come out of the womb, and there is no strength to give birth.
19.4: Perhaps the Lord your God will hear all the words of the chief cupbearer, whom the king of Assyria, his master, has sent to insult the living God, and will punish him for the words the Lord your God has heard. Therefore, offer up a prayer for the remnant that is still left.»
19.5: King Hezekiah's servants went to Isaiah
19.6: And Isaiah said to them, «This is what you shall say to your master. Thus says the Lord: «Do not be afraid of the words that you have heard, by which the servants of the king of Assyria have insulted me.
19.7: »I am putting a spirit in him so that, upon hearing a rumor, he will return to his own country, and I will cause him to fall by the sword in his own country.”
19.8: The chief cupbearer returned and found the king of Assyria attacking Lobna, for he had learned that his master had left Lachis.
19.9: The king of Assyria received news concerning Tharaca, king of Ethiopia: «He has set out to wage war against you.» And he again sent messengers to Hezekiah, saying:
19.10: «Say this to Hezekiah, king of Judah: Do not let your God, in whom you trust, deceive you by saying, ‘Jerusalem will not be given into the hands of the king of Assyria.’”.
19.11: Behold, you have heard what the kings of Assyria did to all the countries, subjecting them to anathema, and you would be delivered.
19.12: Did their gods deliver them, those nations that my fathers destroyed: Goshan, Haran, Reseph, and the sons of Eden who were in Telasar?
19.13: Where are the king of Emath, the king of Arphad, and the king of the city of Sepharvaim, of Anah, and of Ava?»
19.14: Hezekiah received the letter from the messengers and read it. Then Hezekiah went up to the house of the Lord and spread it out before the Lord.
19.15: And Hezekiah prayed before the Lord, saying, «Lord, God of Israel, seated upon the Cherubim, you alone are God over all the kingdoms of the earth, you who made the heavens and the earth.
19.16: Lord, incline your ear and listen; Lord, open your eyes and see. Hear the words of Sennacherib, who has sent the chief cupbearer to insult the living God.
19.17: It is true, Lord, that the kings of Assyria destroyed the nations and devastated their territories.
19.18: and that they threw their gods into the fire, for they were not gods, but works of human hands, wood and stone, and they destroyed them.
19.19: Now, Lord our God, save us from the hand of Sennacherib, and let all the kingdoms of the earth know that you alone, Lord, are God.»
19.20: And Isaiah, son of Amos, sent word to Hezekiah: «Thus says the Lord, God of Israel: The prayer that you have addressed to me concerning Sennacherib, king of Assyria, I have heard.
19.21: This is the word that the Lord has spoken against him: She despises you, she mocks you, the virgin daughter of Zion; she shakes her head behind you, the daughter of Jerusalem.
19.22: Whom have you insulted and outraged? Against whom have you raised your voice and lifted your eyes? Against the Holy One of Israel.
19.23: Through your messengers you have insulted the Lord and said: With my many chariots I have ascended the mountain peaks, the farthest reaches of Lebanon, I will cut down its tallest cedars, its finest cypresses, and I will reach its highest summit, its forest like an orchard.
19.24: I dug and drank foreign waters; with the soles of my feet I will dry up all the rivers of Egypt.
19.25: Have you not heard that I did these things long ago, that I formed them from ancient times? Now I am bringing them to pass, so that you may reduce the fortified cities to heaps of ruins.
19.26: Their inhabitants are powerless, in terror and confusion; they are like the grass of the fields and the tender greenery, like the sod on the roofs, like the wheat that dries up before it is ripe.
19.27: But I know when you sit down, when you go out and when you come in, I know your fury against me.
19.28: Because you are furious with me and your arrogance has reached my ears, I will put my ring in your nostril and my bit in your lips and I will make you return by the way you came.
19.29: And this will be a sign for you: This year you will eat what grows of itself, and in the second year you will eat what springs from itself, but in the third year you will sow and reap, and plant vineyards and eat their fruit.
19.30: What has been saved from the house of Judah, what remains, will still put forth roots underneath and bear fruit above.
19.31: For out of Jerusalem a remnant will go forth, and out of Mount Zion survivors. This is what the zeal of the Lord of hosts will accomplish.
19.32: Therefore this is what the Lord says concerning the king of Assyria: He will not enter this city, nor will he shoot an arrow at it, nor will he present shields to it, nor will he build siege works against it.
19.33: He will return by the way he came and will not enter this city, declares the Lord.
19.34: I will protect this city to save it, for my own sake and for the sake of David, my servant.
19.35: That night the angel of the Lord went out and struck down
19.36: And Sennacherib, king of Assyria, having broken camp, departed and returned and remained in Nineveh.
19.37: As he was worshiping in the house of Nesroch, his god, Adramelech and Sarasar, his sons, struck him with the sword and fled to the land of Ararat. And Esarhaddon, his son, reigned in his place.

20.1: At that time, Hezekiah became ill and was at the point of death. The prophet Isaiah, son of Amos, came to him and said, «This is what the Lord says: Put your house in order, because you are going to die; you will not recover.»
20.2: Hezekiah turned his face to the wall and prayed to the Lord in these words:
20.3: «Remember, O Lord, that I have walked before you faithfully and with wholehearted devotion, and have done what is good in your eyes.» And Hezekiah wept bitterly.
20.4: Isaiah had not yet gone out to the middle court when the word of the Lord came to him, saying:
20.5: Go back and tell Hezekiah, the leader of my people: This is what the Lord, the God of your father David, says: I have heard your prayer and seen your tears; behold, I will heal you. In three days you will go up to the house of the Lord,
20.6: I will add fifteen years to your life. I will deliver you and this city from the hand of the king of Assyria; I will defend this city for my own sake and for the sake of David my servant.»
20.7: Isaiah said, "Take a lump of figs." They took it, applied it to the ulcer, and Hezekiah was healed.
20.8: Hezekiah had said to Isaiah, "What will be the sign that the Lord will heal me and that I will go up to the house of the Lord in three days?"«
20.9: Isaiah said, «This is the sign to you from the Lord, by which you will know that the Lord will do what he has said: Will the shadow go forward ten steps, or will it go back ten steps?»
20.10: Hezekiah replied, "It is a small thing for a shadow to advance ten degrees, but to retreat ten degrees."«
20.11: Then Isaiah the prophet called on the Lord, who made the shadow go back ten steps on the steps of Ahaz, on the steps where it had gone down.
20.12: At that time, Merodach-Baladan, son of Baladan, king of Babylon, sent a letter and gifts to Hezekiah, because he had heard that Hezekiah was sick.
20.13: Hezekiah rejoiced at the arrival of the envoys and showed them all his treasury house, the silver and gold, the spices and precious oil, all his armory and everything that was in his treasuries; there was nothing that Hezekiah did not show them in his house and in all his domains.
20.14: But Isaiah the prophet came to King Hezekiah and said to him, «What did these people say, and where did they come from?» Hezekiah replied, «They came from a distant land, from Babylon.»
20.15: And Isaiah said, «What did they see in your house?» And Hezekiah answered, «They saw everything in my house; there is nothing among my treasures that I did not show them.»
20.16: And Isaiah said to Hezekiah, «Hear the word of the Lord:
20.17: “The days will come when everything in your house and everything your ancestors have stored up until this day will be carried off to Babylon; nothing will be left,” says the Lord.
20.18: And some of your sons, who will come from you, whom you will have fathered, will be taken to become eunuchs in the palace of the king of Babylon.»
20.19: Hezekiah answered Isaiah, «The word of the Lord that you have spoken is good.» And he added, «Yes, for peace and stability will be with me all my days.»
20.20: The rest of the acts of Hezekiah, all his exploits, and how he made the pool and the aqueduct and brought water into the city, are they not written in the book of the Chronicles of the kings of Judah?
20.21: Hezekiah rested with his ancestors, and Manasseh his son reigned in his place.

21.1: Manasseh was twelve years old when he became king and he reigned fifty-five years in Jerusalem. His mother's name was Haphisiba.
21.2: He did what was evil in the sight of the Lord, imitating the abominations of the nations that the Lord had driven out before the children of Israel.
21.3: He rebuilt the sacred places that Hezekiah, his father, had destroyed, he erected altars to Baal, he made an Asherah, as Ahab, king of Israel, had done, and he bowed down before all the host of heaven and served them.
21.4: He built altars in the house of the Lord, of which the Lord had said, «In Jerusalem I will put my name.»
21.5: He built altars for all the host of heaven in the two courts of the house of the Lord.
21.6: He made his son pass through the fire, he practiced augury and divination, he appointed necromancers and sorcerers: thus doing more and more what was evil in the sight of the Lord, so as to anger him.
21.7: He placed the idol of Asherah, which he had made, in the house of which the Lord had said to David and to Solomon his son: «It is in this house and in Jerusalem, which I have chosen out of all the tribes of Israel, that I will put my name forever.
21.8: I will no longer cause the feet of Israel to wander from the land I gave to their ancestors, provided they are careful to observe all that I have commanded them and all the law that my servant Moses prescribed for them.»
21.9: But they did not obey, and Manasseh led them astray, so that they did more evil than all the nations that the Lord had destroyed before the children of Israel.
21.10: Then the Lord spoke through his servants the prophets, saying:
21.11: «Because Manasseh, king of Judah, committed these abominations, because he did worse than all that the Amorites had done before him, and because he also caused Judah to sin through his idols,
21.12: This is what the Lord, the God of Israel, says: Behold, I am going to bring on Jerusalem and on Judah such disasters that both ears will ring when anyone who hears of them.
21.13: I will stretch out over Jerusalem the measuring line of Samaria and the plumb line of the house of Ahab, and I will cleanse Jerusalem like a dish that is cleaned and turned upside down after being cleaned.
21.14: I will abandon the rest of my inheritance and deliver them into the hands of their enemies, and they will become the prey and plunder of all their enemies:
21.15: because they have done what is evil in my sight and have provoked me to anger from the day their ancestors left Egypt until now.»
21.16: Manasseh also shed much innocent blood, until Jerusalem was filled from one end to the other, besides his sins by which he caused Judah to sin, leading them to do what is evil in the sight of the Lord.
21.17: The rest of the acts of Manasseh, all that he did and the sins that he committed, are they not written in the book of the Chronicles of the kings of Judah?
21.18: Manasseh rested with his ancestors and was buried in the garden of his house, in the garden of Uzza. Amun, his son, reigned in his place.
21.19: Amon was twenty-two years old when he became king and he reigned two years in Jerusalem. His mother's name was Messalemeth, daughter of Harus, of Jeteba.
21.20: He did what was evil in the sight of the Lord, just as his father Manasseh had done.,
21.21: He walked in all the ways his father had walked, he served the idols his father had served, and he bowed down before them.,
21.22: He abandoned the Lord, the God of his fathers, and did not walk in the way of the Lord.
21.23: The servants of Amun conspired against him and killed the king in his house.
21.24: But the people of the land struck down all those who had conspired against King Amon, and the people of the land installed Josiah, his son, as king in his place.
21.25: The rest of the acts of Amon and what he did, are they not written in the book of the Chronicles of the Kings of Judah?
21.26: He was buried in his tomb in the garden of Oza, and Josiah, his son, reigned in his place.

22.1: Josiah was eight years old when he became king, and he reigned thirty-one years in Jerusalem. His mother's name was Idida, daughter of Hadai of Bezekath.
22.2: He did what was right in the eyes of the Lord and walked entirely in the way of David his father, and did not turn aside to the right or to the left.
22.3: In the eighteenth year of King Josiah, the king sent to the house of the Lord Shaphan, the secretary, son of Asliah, son of Messulam, saying:
22.4: «"Go up to Helcias, the high priest, and have ready the money that was brought into the house of the Lord and that the gatekeepers collected from the people.
22.5: This money will be given into the hands of those who are carrying out the work, who are appointed overseers in the house of the Lord, and they will give it to those who are working on the construction of the house of the Lord, to repair the breaches in the house,
22.6: to the carpenters, laborers and masons and they will use it to buy the wood and cut stones to repair the house.
22.7: But they will not be held accountable for the money entrusted to them, because they behave with integrity.»
22.8: Then Helkias, the high priest, said to Shaphan, the secretary, "I have found the Book of the Law in the house of the Lord." And Helkias gave the book to Shaphan, who read it.
22.9: Then Saphan, the secretary, returned to the king and reported to him, saying, «Your servants have emptied the money that was in the house and given it to those who are carrying out the work, who are appointed overseers in the house of the Lord.»
22.10: Saphan, the secretary, then made this further communication to the king: "The priest Helcias gave me a book." And Saphan read it aloud to the king.
22.11: When the king heard the words of the Book of the Law, he tore his clothes,
22.12: And he gave this order to the priest Helcias, to Ahicam son of Shaphan, to Ahobor son of Micha, to Shaphan the secretary, and to Asaias, the king's servant:
22.13: «Go and inquire of the Lord for me, for the people, and for all Judah concerning the words of this book that has been found, for great is the anger of the Lord which is kindled against us, because our fathers did not obey the words of this book, by doing all that is commanded us.»
22.14: Helcias the priest, Ahicam, Ahobor, Shaphan, and Asaias went to Holdah the prophetess, the wife of Shellum, son of Tekuah, son of Araas, keeper of the wardrobe; she lived in the Second Quarter of Jerusalem. When they had spoken to her,
22.15: She said to them, «This is what the Lord, the God of Israel, says: Tell the man who sent you to me:
22.16: Thus says the Lord: Behold, I am going to bring disaster on this place and on its inhabitants, according to all the words of the book which the king of Judah has read.
22.17: Because they have forsaken me and have offered incense to other gods, provoking me to anger with all the works of their hands, my anger has been kindled against this place and will not be quenched.
22.18: And you shall say to the king of Judah, who sent you to inquire of the Lord: Thus says the Lord, the God of Israel: As for the words that you have heard,
22.19: because your heart has repented and you have humbled yourself before the Lord when you heard what I have spoken against this place and against its inhabitants, namely that they shall be an object of horror and of cursing, and because you have torn your clothes and wept before me, I also have heard you, oracle of the Lord.
22.20: Therefore, behold, I will gather you to your fathers, you will be gathered in peace to your tomb, and your eyes will not see all the calamities that I will bring upon this place.» They reported this answer to the king.

23.1: The king sent and gathered to him all the elders of Judah and Jerusalem.
23.2: And the king went up to the house of the Lord, with all the men of Judah and all the inhabitants of Jerusalem, the priests, the prophets, and all the people, from the least to the greatest, and he read before them all the words of the book of the covenant, which had been found in the house of the Lord.
23.3: The king, standing on the platform, concluded the covenant before the Lord, pledging to follow the Lord and to observe his precepts, his ordinances and his laws, with all his heart and with all his soul, fulfilling the words of this covenant, which are written in this book, and all the people acquiesced to this covenant.
23.4: The king ordered Helkias, the high priest, the priests of the second order, and those who guarded the gate, to cast out of the temple of the Lord all the utensils that had been made for Baal, for Astarte, and for all the host of heaven, and he burned them outside Jerusalem in the fields of the Kidron and had the ashes carried to Bethel.
23.5: He drove out the idol priests, appointed by the kings of Judah, who burned incense at the holy places in the cities of Judah and around Jerusalem, and those who offered incense to Baal, to the sun, to the moon, to the twelve signs, and to all the host of heaven.
23.6: He removed the Asherah from the house of the Lord and carried it out of Jerusalem to the Kidron Valley, burned it in the Kidron Valley, and, having reduced it to ashes, he threw the ashes on the graves of the people.
23.7: He tore down the houses of the prostitutes that were in the house of the Lord and where the women wove tents for Astarte.
23.8: He brought in all the priests from the cities of Judah: he defiled the sacred places where the priests had burned incense, from Gibeah to Beersheba, and he overturned the sacred places at the gates, the one that was at the entrance of the gate of Joshua, the ruler of the city, and the one that was to the left of the city gate.
23.9: However, the priests of the holy places did not go up to the altar of the Lord in Jerusalem, but they ate unleavened bread in the midst of their brothers.
23.10: The king defiled Topheth, in the valley of the sons of Ennom, so that no one would make their son or daughter pass through the fire in honor of Moloch.
23.11: He removed the horses that the kings of Judah had dedicated to the sun at the entrance of the house of the Lord, near the chamber of the eunuch Nathan-Melech, which was in the outbuildings, and he burned the chariots of the sun in the fire.
23.12: The king destroyed the altars that were on the roof of the upper room of Ahaz, which the kings of Judah had made, and the altars that Manasseh had made in the two courts of the house of the Lord, and from there he ran to throw their ashes into the Kidron Valley.
23.13: The king defiled the holy places that were opposite Jerusalem, on the right side of the Mount of Perdition, which Solomon, king of Israel, had built at Ashtoreth, the abomination of the Sidonians, at Hamos, the abomination of the Ammonites,
23.14: He broke the stelae and cut down the Asherahs and filled the place they occupied with human bones.
23.15: Likewise, the altar that was at Bethel and the high place that Jeroboam, son of Nebat, had made, who had caused Israel to sin, he destroyed this altar and the high place, he burned the high place and reduced it to ashes and he burned the Asherah.
23.16: Josiah, having turned and having seen the tombs that were there in the mountain, sent and took the bones from the tombs and burned them on the altar and defiled it, according to the word of the Lord spoken by the man of God who had announced these things.
23.17: He then said, «What is this monument that I see?» The people of the town told him, «It is the tomb of the man of God who came from Judah and who told us about the things you did against the altar at Bethel.»
23.18: And he said, «Leave him alone; let no one disturb his bones.» So they left his bones undisturbed, along with the bones of the prophet who had come from Samaria.
23.19: Josiah also destroyed all the houses of the sacred places, which were in the cities of Samaria and which had been made by the kings of Israel, provoking the Lord to anger, he did to them exactly as he had done to Bethel.
23.20: He sacrificed on the altars all the priests of the holy places who were there and burned human bones there, then he returned to Jerusalem.
23.21: The king gave this order to all the people: «Celebrate the Passover in honor of the Lord your God, as it is written in the book of the covenant.»
23.22: No Passover like this had been celebrated since the time of the judges who judged Israel and during all the days of the kings of Israel and the kings of Judah.
23.23: It was in the eighteenth year of King Josiah that this Passover was celebrated in honor of the Lord in Jerusalem.
23.24: Josiah also removed the necromancers and sorcerers, as well as the teraphim, the idols, and all the abominations that were seen in the land of Judah and in Jerusalem, in order to fulfill the words of the Law, written in the book that the priest Helkiah had found in the house of the Lord.
23.25: Before Josiah there was no king like him who turned to the Lord with all his heart, with all his soul, and with all his strength, according to all the law of Moses, and after him there has been no one like him.
23.26: However, the Lord did not turn from the fierceness of his great anger, because his anger was kindled against Judah, because of the provocations with which Manasseh had angered him.
23.27: And the Lord said, «I will also remove Judah from my sight, as I removed Israel, and I will reject this city Jerusalem, which I had chosen, and this house of which I said, »My name shall be there.’”
23.28: The rest of the acts of Josiah and all that he did, are they not written in the book of the Chronicles of the Kings of Judah?
23.29: In his time, Pharaoh Necho, king of Egypt, marched against the king of Assyria towards the Euphrates River. King Josiah marched to meet him, and Pharaoh killed him at Mageddo as soon as he saw him.
23.30: His servants carried him dead from Mageddo on a chariot and, having brought him to Jerusalem, they buried him in his tomb. And the people of the land took Jehoahaz son of Josiah, they anointed him and made him king in place of his father.
23.31: Jehoahaz was twenty-three years old when he became king and he reigned three months in Jerusalem. His mother's name was Amital, daughter of Jeremiah, from Lobna.
23.32: He did what was evil in the sight of the Lord, just as his fathers had done.
23.33: Pharaoh Necho bound him in Rebla, in the land of Emath, so that he would no longer reign in Jerusalem, and he imposed on the land a tax of one hundred talents of silver and one talent of gold.
23.34: Pharaoh Necho appointed Eliakim, son of Josiah, as king in place of his father Josiah, and changed his name to Jehoiakim. Jehoahaz, whom he had captured, went to Egypt and died there.
23.35: Joakim gave Pharaoh the silver and gold, but he taxed the country to provide the sum demanded by Pharaoh: each one according to what was fixed for him, raised the silver and gold from the people of the land, to give it to Pharaoh-Necho.
23.36: Joakim was twenty-five years old when he became king and he reigned eleven years in Jerusalem. His mother's name was Zebida, daughter of Phaedaias, from Ruma.
23.37: He did what was evil in the sight of the Lord, just as his fathers had done.

24.1: In his time, Nebuchadnezzar, king of Babylon, went on campaign and Jehoiakim was subject to him for three years, but he rebelled against him again.
24.2: The Lord sent against Jehoiakim bands of Chaldeans, bands of Syrians, bands of Moabites and bands of Ammonites, he sent them against Judah to destroy it, according to the word of the Lord which he had spoken through the mouth of his servants the prophets.
24.3: It was solely at the Lord's command that this happened to Judah, to remove them from his presence because of all the sins committed by Manasseh
24.4: And because of the innocent blood that Manasseh had shed, to the point of filling Jerusalem with innocent blood. That is why the Lord did not want to forgive.
24.5: The rest of the acts of Jehoiakim and all that he did, is it not written in the book of the Chronicles of the Kings of Judah?
24.6: Joakim rested with his fathers, and Joachim his son reigned in his place.
24.7: The king of Egypt did not leave his country again, because the king of Babylon had taken everything that belonged to the king of Egypt, from the Wadi of Egypt to the river Euphrates.
24.8: Joachim was eighteen years old when he became king and he reigned for three months in Jerusalem. His mother was named Nohestah, daughter of Elnathan, of Jerusalem.
24.9: He did what was evil in the sight of the Lord, just as his father had done.
24.10: At that time, the servants of Nebuchadnezzar, king of Babylon, went up against Jerusalem and the city was besieged.
24.11: Nebuchadnezzar, king of Babylon, came before the city while his servants were besieging it.
24.12: Then Jehoiachin, king of Judah, went out to the king of Babylon, with his mother, his servants, his officials and his eunuchs, and the king of Babylon took him prisoner in the eighth year of his reign.
24.13: He carried away from there all the treasures of the house of the Lord and the treasures of the king's house, and he broke all the gold utensils that Solomon king of Israel had made in the temple of the Lord, as the Lord had foretold.
24.14: He took all Jerusalem captive, all the leaders and all the valiant men, ten thousand captives, with all the craftsmen and blacksmiths, leaving only the poor people of the land.
24.15: He transported Jehoiachin to Babylon and he carried captive from Jerusalem to Babylon the king's mother, the king's wives and his eunuchs and the nobles of the land.
24.16: Moreover, all the warriors, numbering seven thousand, as well as the artisans and blacksmiths, numbering one thousand, all valiant men fit for war: the king of Babylon took them captive to Babylon.
24.17: And the king of Babylon appointed Mattanias, his uncle, as king in place of Jehoiachin, and changed his name to Zedekiah.
24.18: Zedekiah was twenty-one years old when he became king, and he reigned eleven years in Jerusalem. His mother's name was Amital, daughter of Jeremiah, from Lobna.
24.19: He did what was evil in the eyes of the Lord, imitating everything that Joakim had done.
24.20: This happened in Jerusalem and Judah because of the Lord's anger, until he cast them out of his presence. And Zedekiah rebelled against the king of Babylon.

25.1: In the ninth year of Zedekiah’s reign, in the tenth month, on the tenth day of the month, Nebuchadnezzar, king of Babylon, came with his whole army against Jerusalem and set up camp before it, and walls were built all around it.
25.2: The city was besieged until the eleventh year of Zedekiah.
25.3: On the ninth day of the month, as the famine worsened in the city and there was no more bread for the people of the land,
25.4: A breach was made in the city, and all the soldiers fled by night through the gate between the two walls, near the king's garden, while the Chaldeans surrounded the city. The king then headed towards the Arabah.
25.5: But the Chaldean army pursued the king and caught up with him on the plains of Jericho, and his entire army scattered away from him.
25.6: Having seized the king, they brought him up to the king of Babylon at Rebla and a sentence was pronounced against him.
25.7: They slaughtered Zedekiah's sons before his eyes. Then Nebuchadnezzar blinded Zedekiah and bound him with two bronze chains and took him to Babylon.
25.8: In the fifth month, on the seventh day of the month, in the nineteenth year of the reign of Nebuchadnezzar, king of Babylon, Nebuzardan, captain of the guard, servant of the king of Babylon, came to Jerusalem.
25.9: He burned down the house of the Lord, the king's house, and all the houses of Jerusalem; he burned down all the great houses.
25.10: The entire Chaldean army, which was with the captain of the guards, demolished the walls forming the enclosure of Jerusalem.
25.11: Nebuzardan, captain of the guards, took captive the rest of the people who had remained in the city, the deserters who had surrendered to the king of Babylon, and the rest of the multitude.
25.12: The captain of the guards left some of the poor of the country as winegrowers and farmers.
25.13: The Chaldeans broke down the bronze pillars that were in the house of the Lord, as well as the bases and the bronze sea that were in the house of the Lord, and they carried the bronze away to Babylon.
25.14: They took the pots, shovels, knives, cups and all the bronze utensils used for serving.
25.15: The captain of the guards also took the censers and cups, both those made of gold and those made of silver.
25.16: As for the two pillars, the sea, and the bases that Solomon had made in the house of the Lord, there was no need to weigh the bronze of all these utensils.
25.17: The height of one column was eighteen cubits and there was on top of it a bronze capital and the height of the capital was three cubits and there was all around the capital a trellis and pomegranates, all of bronze, it was the same of the second column with the trellis.
25.18: The captain of the guards took Saraias, the high priest, Zephaniah, a priest of the second rank, and the three gatekeepers.
25.19: In the city, he took an officer who commanded the soldiers, five men who were part of the king's privy council and who were found in the city, the secretary of the army commander in charge of enlisting the people of the country, and sixty men from the people of the country who were in the city.
25.20: Having captured them, Nebuzardan, captain of the guards, led them to the king of Babylon at Rebla.
25.21: And the king of Babylon struck them down and put them to death at Rebla in the land of Emath. So Judah was taken captive from its land.
25.22: As for the people who remained in the land of Judah whom Nebuchadnezzar, king of Babylon, left there, he gave to them as governor Gedaliah, son of Ahicam, son of Shaphan.
25.23: When all the commanders of the troops had learned, they and their men, that the king of Babylon had appointed Gedaliah as governor, they came to Gedaliah at Maspha, namely: Ishmael son of Nathaniah, Johanan son of Karea, Saraiah son of Thanehumeth of Netophah and Jezoniah son of the Maachathite, they and their men.
25.24: Godolias swore to them and their men, saying, «Do not fear the servants of the Chaldeans; remain in the land, serve the king of Babylon, and all will be well with you.»
25.25: But in the seventh month, Ishmael, son of Nathanias, son of Elishama, of the royal line, came with ten men and they struck down Gedaliah, as well as the Jews and Chaldeans who were with him at Maspha.
25.26: Then all the people, from the least to the greatest, and the commanders of the troops, arose and went to Egypt, because they were afraid of the Chaldeans.
25.27: In the thirty-seventh year of the captivity of Jehoiachin king of Judah, in the twelfth month, on the twenty-seventh day of the month, Evil-Merodach king of Babylon, in the year of his accession, lifted up the head of Jehoiachin king of Judah and brought him out of prison.
25.28: He spoke kindly to him and placed his throne above the thrones of the kings who were with him in Babylon.
25.29: He made him change his prison clothes and Joachim always ate in his presence, all the rest of his life.
25.30: As for its upkeep, its perpetual upkeep, the king provided for it every day, throughout his entire life.
